claims.claim: At high SOC, lithium ion cells tend to fail via flaming, overpressure, and flaring, which can be a forceful event with torch-like flames involving large amounts of flammable gas.
claims.quote: At a high SOC, lithium ion cells tend to fail via flaming, overpressure, and flaring. This can be a forceful event with torch-like flames, which involves a large amount of flammable gas.

claims.claim: Non-flaming venting tends to occur at a lower SOC, consisting of gas or aerosol ejected through overpressure venting.
claims.quote: Non-flaming venting tends to occur at a lower SOC. The failure event will consist of an amount of gas or aerosol ejected through overpressure venting.

claims.claim: The mean Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) of lithium ion cell vent gas was determined to be on the order of 10%, based on 21 cell varieties tested at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: Archibald and EPRI (2021) determined lithium ion cell vent gas statistical properties for 21 varieties and concluded that the mean Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) was on the order of 10%. It shall be said that Archibald used cells at 100% SOC.

claims.claim: In a 40-ft ISO standard container BESS, a single 94-Ah cell provides enough flammable gas during thermal runaway for a partial volume deflagration to occur.
claims.quote: EPRI established in a recent BESS explosion hazards technical bulletin (EPRI, 2021) that in a 40-ft ISO standard container BESS, a single 94-Ah cell would provide enough flammable gas during thermal runaway for a partial volume deflagration to occur.

claims.claim: HF gas production is directly proportional to the electrical energy stored in the cell or battery and can be conservatively estimated at 200 mg of HF per Wh.
claims.quote: The HF gas production is directly proportional to the electrical energy stored in the cell or battery and can be conservatively estimated with 200 mg of HF/ Wh (Larsson et al., 2017).

claims.claim: A thermal runaway event with fire lowers the probability of large and catastrophic explosions because flammable gases liberated during propagating thermal runaway are partially consumed in the fire.
claims.quote: a thermal runaway with fire (i.e., a burning reaction) lowers the probability of large and catastrophic explosions. This fact should be considered when considering the implementation alternative suppression systems such as clean agents.

claims.claim: Clean agent suppression systems can extinguish flames but may indirectly contribute to the accumulation of flammable gases inside the enclosure, increasing explosion risk.
claims.quote: Those systems can be effective in extinguishing flaming (Wang et al., 2016), but they may indirectly contribute to the accumulation of flammable gases inside the enclosure.

claims.claim: HF has the lowest IDLH value among the commonly reported toxic gases from lithium-ion battery failures, with an IDLH of 30 ppm, compared to CO at 1200 ppm, SO2 at 100 ppm, NO at 100 ppm, HCl at 50 ppm, and HCN at 50 ppm.
claims.quote: In terms of IDLH, HF is the most dangerous gas out of the ones listed above, with an IDLH value of 30 ppm... Both HCN and HCl have an IDLH value of 50 ppm... Both SO2 and NO have an IDLH value of 100 ppm, and CO has an IDLH value of 1200 ppm.

claims.claim: In burning battery failure simulations, the most dangerous phase in terms of downwind toxicity did not occur at peak mass flow rate but at approximately 1 kg/s, because above 1 kg/s increased HRR raises plume buoyancy and reduces safety distance more than the increase in toxic gas mass flow rate extends it.
claims.quote: for the burning case, the 'worst-case' timestep corresponded to a mass flow rate of approximately 1 kg/s rather than to the peak. This means that the most dangerous phase (in terms of downwind toxicity) of a battery fire may not occur at its peak... When the total mass flow rate is smaller than 1 kg/s, the second effect dominates, and the safety distance increases with the mass flow rate. However, beyond 1 kg/s, the first effect dominates, and the safety distance drops with the total mass flow rate.

claims.claim: For non-burning battery failure simulations, safety distance did not monotonically increase with wind speed; the peak safety distance occurred at 8 km/h (5 mph), with higher wind speeds reducing safety distance due to increased dilution.
claims.quote: The main difference with the burning cases is that safety distance did not monotonically increase with wind speed. For the 100% SOC cases, Fig. 15 shows safety distance as a function of wind speed for all battery energies. The peak safety distance occurs at 8 km/h (5 mph) for all cases. Up to 8 km/h (5 mph), the increasing wind speeds increase advection... However, as the wind speed increases further, the trend is reversed. The increasing wind speeds increase dilution, which reduces the safety distances.

claims.claim: For burning battery failure simulations, the variation in toxic gas yields was the most impactful parameter on safety distance, with higher yields producing greater safety distances, and HF yield was the dominant factor because HF set the safety distance in all burning simulation cases.
claims.quote: For the burning case, the most impactful parameters were the toxic gas yields. Unsurprisingly, the higher the yields are, the greater the safety distance is... For all burning simulations, HF was the gas setting the safety distance. This means that HF was the gas that required the largest distance to drop below its IDLH.

claims.claim: EV and ICEV fires simulated using RISE experimental data produced similar safety distances of approximately 4–5 m, but the EV produced substantially more HF, resulting in much higher HF concentrations downwind compared to the ICEV.
claims.quote: There were no significant differences in terms of safety distance between the two simulations... For both simulations, the maximum safety distance was around 4–5 m... For the ICEV simulation, the HF concentration did not reach IDLH. The HF plume for the EV simulation extended outside of the room.

claims.claim: The maximum peak overpressure (PMax) of DMC is 31% higher than that of BVG, measured at 393 K and 0.1 MPa in an 8 L cylindrical vessel.
claims.quote: The maximum Pex of DMC is 31% higher than that of BVG. This is mainly due to the presence of inert gases in BVG, which can suppress explosions, and DMC has higher combustion heat than BVG

claims.claim: As xDMC increased from 0.1 to 0.5, PMax and (dp/dt)Max increased by 12.2% and 17.7% respectively, while increasing xDMC from 0.6 to 0.9 yielded only 5.5% and 5.6% increases respectively.
claims.quote: When xDMC increased from 0.1 to 0.5, PMax and (dp/dt)Max increased by 12.2% and 17.7%, respectively. However, PMax and (dp/dt)Max increased by 5.5% and 5.6%, respectively, during the increment of xDMC from 0.6 to 0.9.

claims.claim: The LFL of DMC/BVG mixtures decreases approximately linearly as initial temperature increases from 363 K to 423 K, with larger xDMC values showing greater LFL reduction over this temperature range.
claims.quote: When the initial temperature was increased from 363 K to 423 K, LFL, corresponding to xDMC = 0.1, xDMC = 0.5, and xDMC = 0.9, decreased by 4.85%, 9.73%, and 12.82%, respectively.

claims.claim: The LOC of DMC/BVG mixtures increases linearly with increasing xDMC (R² = 0.97) because 1 mol DMC consumes approximately 5.5 times more oxygen than 1 mol BVG for complete combustion.
claims.quote: As xDMC increases, the LOC of the mixture shows a linear increase trend (R2 = 0.97)... the oxygen consumption of the DMC is about 5.5 times that of the BVG, which explains the phenomenon of the mixture LOC increasing with the increase of DMC fraction.

claims.claim: When xDMC is less than 0.3, LOC is lower than LFL and combustible concentration is the priority safety concern; when xDMC exceeds 0.3, LOC is higher than LFL and oxygen concentration becomes the preferred control parameter.
claims.quote: the LOC is lower than the LFL until xDMC = 0.3, and the combustibles' concentration should be give priority to in the system. When xDMC>0.3, the LOC is higher than the LFL and the O2 concentration is preferred to be considered.
